# Summary

The Liberal Democrats are calling on Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer to prevent King Charles III and Queen Camilla from making a planned state visit to the United States in late April, citing Donald Trump's criticism of Britain's response to Iran. The dispute stems from the UK's initial refusal to allow American planes to use British bases for bombing raids on Iran, though Britain has since authorized the US to conduct "defensive" strikes on Iranian missile sites. Trump has publicly criticized Starmer, calling him "no Winston Churchill," while the prime minister has defended his approach as consistent with Britain's principles and security interests. A cabinet minister rejected the Liberal Democrats' position, stating it is not their role to decide what the King should do.

articles.title: Horse racing trainer guilty of hockey stick assault
articles.summary:
# Summary

Welsh racehorse trainer Evan Williams, 54, has been found guilty of assault with intent to cause grievous bodily harm against 72-year-old Martin Dandridge, whom he struck repeatedly with a hockey stick in December 2024. The incident occurred when Dandridge was walking his dog near Williams's training facility in Llancarfan, Vale of Glamorgan; Williams claimed he mistook Dandridge for someone "lamping" (using bright lights to hunt animals) and that Dandridge's injuries resulted from falling due to his dog. The jury deliberated for one hour 45 minutes before finding Williams guilty, breaking Dandridge's arm during the assault. Williams will be sentenced on April 14 at Cardiff Crown Court, with reports to be prepared ahead of sentencing.

Four-year-old Caitlyn competed at Crufts with her miniature schnauzer Zeeva and received a "highly commended" award, finishing fifth in the veteran bitch class—outperforming her mother Lydia who competed in the same category with a different dog. articles.title: Four big calls Borthwick could make to revive England

# Summary

England head coach Steve Borthwick faces key selection decisions ahead of the Six Nations finale against France after the team's continued poor performance, including a loss to Italy. Additionally, Borthwick could reassess England's defensive tactics with attack coaches Lee Blackett and Richard Wigglesworth to improve the team's scoring efficiency, as England has managed less than two points per 22-meter entry in recent matches.

# Summary

Celtic defeated Rangers 3-2 on penalties in a Scottish Cup match at Ibrox on Sunday, with Tomas Cvancara scoring the decisive penalty after 120 goalless minutes. This incident occurred during Celtic's first full away allocation at Ibrox since 2018, raising questions about future ticket distributions for Old Firm derbies.

articles.title: G7 to take 'necessary measures' to support energy supplies
articles.summary:
# Summary

G7 nations pledged to take "necessary measures" to support global energy supplies following escalating conflict in the Middle East, though a meeting between G7 finance ministers and the International Energy Agency ended without agreement to release strategic oil reserves. Oil prices surged to nearly $120 a barrel due to fears of prolonged supply disruptions, with approximately one-fifth of global oil shipments typically transiting through the Strait of Hormuz now halted. Global stock markets fell in response, and analysts warn that prolonged conflict could push oil prices to $120-$150 per barrel, potentially causing demand destruction and further inflation pressures that may delay interest rate cuts by central banks.

articles.title: Hundreds of teenagers report for duty as Croatia reinstates conscription
articles.summary:
# Summary

Croatia has reinstated compulsory military service for the first time since 2008, with hundreds of teenagers reporting for duty. The first batch of approximately 800 conscripts will undergo two months of military training at barracks across the country, covering traditional skills alongside modern training in drone control and cyberwarfare. The reinstatement was prompted by regional instability, particularly the proximity of the war in Ukraine, with Croatia aiming to train 4,000 recruits annually. The move has prompted neighboring countries, including Slovenia and Serbia, to consider or announce plans to reinstate their own military service requirements.

# Summary

A magistrate granted a temporary non-publication order, though noting his mother's name had already been widely published, and News Corp Australia indicated it would likely challenge the order. Akram faces 59 charges including 15 counts of murder and has not yet entered a plea; his father was killed by police at the scene of the attack.

# Summary

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney has called three by-elections for April 13 that could give his Liberal Party a majority government. The Liberals currently hold 169 seats—three short of a majority—and winning all three contested seats would secure the 172 needed to govern. Two of the by-elections are in Toronto in traditionally Liberal strongholds, while the third is a closely watched rematch in the Montreal suburb of Terrebonne, where the Liberals won by a single vote in 2025. The vacant seats resulted from the resignations of Chrystia Freeland and Bill Blair, who left for other roles.

articles.title: Bag of cannabis and cash accidentally donated to NZ charity shop
articles.summary:
# Summary

Two teenagers in New Zealand were arrested after accidentally leaving a backpack containing 43.2g of cannabis and NZ$3,700 (approximately $2,200) in cash at a charity shop in Southland on February 18. A shop volunteer discovered the bag in the donation area after noticing a strong smell and alerted police. When officers searched the teenagers' vehicle, they also found an air pistol, a police scanner, and additional cash. Due to New Zealand's Youth Court confidentiality rules, officials have not released details about the charges or court appearances.

articles.title: Guinea opposition leader urges 'direct resistance' after 40 parties dissolved
articles.summary:
# Summary

Guinea's authorities dissolved 40 political parties, citing non-compliance with legal requirements—a charge the parties deny. Opposition leader Cellou Dalein Diallo, whose own party was among those dissolved, called for "direct resistance" to President Mamady Doumbouya, stating that democratic change is no longer possible. The dissolution comes two months before legislative elections and targets parties that could challenge Doumbouya's consolidation of power following his 2021 coup and subsequent election as president.

articles.title: India offered sanctuary to Iranian ship three days before US sank it
articles.summary:
# Summary

India granted permission on March 1 for three Iranian naval ships to dock at Indian ports, three days before the US sank the Iranian warship Iris Dena on March 4 in international waters off Sri Lanka's coast, killing at least 87 crew members. The three ships—Iris Dena, Iris Bushehr, and Iris Lavan—had participated in a military exercise in India between February 15-25 before leaving Indian waters on February 25. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth characterized the sinking as a demonstration of American military capability, though Iran's Foreign Minister called it an "atrocity" conducted without warning in international waters.

articles.title: Istanbul's mayor on trial for corruption charges
articles.summary:
# Summary

Istanbul's Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu appeared in court as the central defendant in a mass corruption trial, facing over 140 charges including corruption and running a criminal organization, with prosecutors seeking a jail term of up to 2,430 years. Imamoglu and his Republican People's Party deny wrongdoing and claim the trial is politically motivated, accusing President Erdogan of launching a crackdown. The trial began chaotically on Monday when Imamoglu contested the judge's procedural decision, leading to a recess just 15 minutes after proceedings started. Human rights organizations have characterized the prosecution as politically motivated and raising serious fair trial concerns, while Imamoglu was arrested in March after being named as his party's 2028 presidential candidate.

articles.title: N Korea cancels Pyongyang Marathon for 'some reasons'
articles.summary:
# Summary

North Korea has cancelled the 2026 Pyongyang Marathon scheduled for April 5, citing unspecified reasons. The cancellation was announced by Koryo Tours, the official tour partner, which said the decision was made "at a level above the organisers" without further explanation. The annual marathon, established in 1981 to celebrate North Korea's founding leader Kim Il Sung's birth, had just resumed last year after a five-year suspension due to the Covid pandemic. Koryo Tours confirmed that all deposits will be refunded and participants can either retain their deposit for future events or North Korea tours.

articles.title: Australians must prove they are over 18 to access porn under new laws
articles.summary:
# Summary

Australia has implemented new laws requiring users to verify they are over 18 before accessing adult content, including pornography, R-rated video games, and sexually explicit AI chatbots. Platforms must introduce stricter age-verification checks using methods such as facial recognition, digital IDs, and credit card details, with companies facing fines for breaches. However, experts warn the laws will have limited effectiveness, as digitally savvy young people can use VPNs, parental credentials, or access unregulated overseas sites to circumvent the restrictions, while also raising concerns about data privacy for all users.

articles.title: Rapper-politician Balendra Shah unseats Nepal's ex-PM as he heads for victory
articles.summary:
# Summary

Rapper Balendra Shah, 35, has defeated former Nepalese Prime Minister Sharma KP Oli in his parliamentary constituency with 68,348 votes compared to Oli's 18,734. Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is on course to win the general election with a majority in directly elected seats according to partial results. The victory represents a generational shift, as Shah and his party have gained support from Gen Z voters frustrated with Nepal's traditional political establishment and issues like unemployment and forced migration.

articles.title: War fuels debate in Cyprus over UK military bases
articles.summary:
# Summary

A drone strike on Britain's RAF Akrotiri airbase in Cyprus, attributed to Iranian-backed Hezbollah, has intensified debate over the UK's military presence on the island. Protesters demonstrated outside Cyprus' presidential palace demanding the removal of British bases, arguing they occupy the country similar to Turkish occupation of northern Cyprus. The UK Ministry of Defence stated the bases are crucial for regional security and have recently supported operations against Iranian threats, while Cyprus' government emphasized the base was targeted, not Cyprus itself. Cyprus' Foreign Minister indicated the issue of British bases warrants further discussion, though renegotiating the 1960 treaty establishing them would be complex, requiring agreement from the UK, Greece, Turkey, and both Cypriot communities.

articles.title: Boy, 12, among six dead as tornadoes hit Michigan and Oklahoma
articles.summary:
# Summary

At least six people died and more than a dozen were injured when tornadoes struck Michigan and Oklahoma on Friday, including a 12-year-old boy named Silas Anderson who died in Edwardsburg, Michigan. Four deaths occurred in southern Michigan and two in Oklahoma, with storms causing widespread damage including destroyed homes, uprooted trees, downed power lines, and damaged buildings. The National Weather Service warned that severe weather could continue into the weekend with thunderstorms and flash flooding expected from the Great Plains to Texas. Both Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer and Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt declared states of emergency in their respective affected counties to coordinate emergency response and recovery efforts.

articles.title: Swiss reject right-wing plan to cut licence fee for public broadcaster
articles.summary:
# Summary

Swiss voters rejected a right-wing initiative to reduce the public broadcaster's annual licence fee from 335 Swiss francs to 200 francs, with only 38% supporting the proposal. The Swiss People's Party had argued the fee was too high given rising living costs, but the government and other parliamentary parties opposed the cut, citing the need to maintain coverage across Switzerland's four languages and ensure adequate foreign news and sports reporting. In a separate referendum, approximately 70% of voters backed the government's counter-proposal to enshrine cash availability in the constitution, with the Swiss National Bank guaranteeing the supply of cash.

articles.title: Spain's migrants welcome amnesty: 'It will help us in every way'
articles.summary:
# Summary

Spain's government has announced a migrant regularization scheme that will provide at least 500,000 undocumented migrants with one-year renewable residency visas, with applications open from April to June 2024. Applicants must prove five months of residency and have no criminal record. The government cites both humanitarian reasons and economic necessity, as Spain's central bank estimates the country will need approximately 25 million migrants over the next three decades to sustain its economy and social security system. While businesses and migrant advocacy groups support the measure, it faces criticism from right-wing opposition parties who argue it will encourage more illegal immigration.
